title: A menu is not a paragraph with bars
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the menu instead of fixing it. Bring the code back.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order.
hint2.not-closed: There are four levels nested here: <nav>, <ul>, <li>, <a>. Each one closes.
hint3.not-closed:
Whatever you opened last, you close first:
  • …
    • .
hint4.not-closed:
Three links means three
  • and three
    • . And one and inside each.
why.no-nav: The menu is not a menu yet. Three things must nest: <nav> on the outside, a <ul> inside it, and an <li> with a link for each page. Miss any one of them and the code does not say "this is the site's menu".
hint2.no-nav: Wrapping the paragraph in <nav> is not enough. The links must be in a list too.
hint3.no-nav:
• Home
— four tags, one inside the other. One menu item.
hint4.no-nav: Delete the paragraph. In its place: <nav>, then a <ul>, then one <li> for each of the three links.
why.empty-link: There is a link with no words in it. An empty link in a menu is the most useless thing on the page.
why.manual-bars: The bars are still there. Remember the lesson about lists? You are drawing something again instead of saying it. Separators between menu items are CSS — not your job here.
hint2.manual-bars: Search for the | character in your code. Not one should remain.
hint3.manual-bars: You drew the bars because the links were glued together in one paragraph. Now each sits in its own <li> — nothing glues them.
hint4.manual-bars: Remove the three | characters. The list separates the items on its own now.
why.still-p: The old paragraph is still inside the <nav>. You do not wrap the mess — you replace it. A paragraph says "this is text"; a menu is not text, it is a list of links.
hint2.still-p:
Look for a

inside your

. It does not belong there.
hint3.still-p:
…

— that is a paragraph in a navigation. It should be a list in a navigation.
why.no-href: A link in the menu has no address, or it is empty. A menu that leads nowhere is not a menu.
why.lost-h1: The heading is gone. The task is the menu, not deleting things.
blocks.0.text: Your site now has several pages. It needs a menu, so a person can move between them.
blocks.1.text: You built it the obvious way: a paragraph with links and bars between them. Run it. It works. It looks like a menu.
blocks.2.text: But it is not a menu
blocks.3.text: Your code says: "here is a paragraph, and inside it there are three links and some bars". It does not say "here is this site's menu".
blocks.4.text: The difference is not cosmetic. A blind person cannot skip the menu, because they do not know it is a menu. Google does not understand your site's structure. And you typed the bars by hand — drawing again, instead of saying.
blocks.7.text: <nav> says: this is navigation. <ul> says: these are things of one kind. Together: a menu.
blocks.8.text: The bars are gone. If you want separators — that is CSS. If you want the menu horizontal — CSS again. You said what it IS; how it looks, you will decide later.
blocks.10.text: Your first reaction is "why a list, I do not want bullets". Bullets are appearance, and you choose by meaning.
blocks.11.text: A menu is several things of one kind. That is a list. Their order is not random — you decided what comes first.
blocks.12.text: And the most practical part: a blind person hears "list of three items" and knows how big the menu is before entering it.
blocks.14.text: You can have more than one <nav> on a page: the main menu at the top, a second one in the footer, a sidebar. Each is navigation.
blocks.15.text: But not every link is navigation. A link in the middle of a sentence is just a link. <nav> is for groups of links that move you around the site.
blocks.16.text: Your turn. Build the menu properly.
